questions.question: Can you tell me how often you take tacrolimus and how you should space out the doses?
questions.source_quote: You take it twice a day, about twelve hours apart — so think morning and evening, at the same times each day.
questions.expected_answer: Twice a day, about 12 hours apart, at the same times each day (morning and evening).

questions.question: What did we say about taking tacrolimus with or without food?
questions.source_quote: Just be consistent — if you take it with food, always take it with food.
questions.expected_answer: Be consistent — always take it the same way, either always with food or always without.

questions.question: Is there any food or drink you need to avoid with tacrolimus, and why?
questions.source_quote: And do not take it with grapefruit or grapefruit juice, that can raise the level in your blood to a dangerous point.
questions.expected_answer: Avoid grapefruit and grapefruit juice because it can raise the tacrolimus level to a dangerous point.

questions.question: What would you do if you missed a dose of tacrolimus?
questions.source_quote: So never double up if you miss a dose — just call us.
questions.expected_answer: Do not double up on the dose; call the care team.

questions.question: What should you do if you throw up right after taking your medicine?
questions.source_quote: Also call us, don't just retake it on your own.
questions.expected_answer: Call the care team; do not retake the dose on your own.

questions.question: What changes at home would tell you the tacrolimus might be affecting your kidneys?
questions.source_quote: If you're urinating a lot less, or your feet and ankles swell up, let us know.
questions.expected_answer: Urinating much less than usual, or swelling in the feet and ankles — call the team.

questions.question: Can you tell me what CellCept does and how often you take it?
questions.source_quote: Yes, they're a team. CellCept is usually taken every 8 hours.
questions.expected_answer: CellCept works with tacrolimus to calm the immune system so it doesn't attack; taken every 8 hours.

questions.question: Why will you be taking the antibiotic Bactrim?
questions.source_quote: That's why you'll also be on an antibiotic called Bactrim to prevent a specific pneumonia.
questions.expected_answer: To prevent a specific type of pneumonia, since the medicines suppress the immune system.

questions.question: When and where do you need to wear a mask?
questions.source_quote: When your counts are low you'll wear a mask in public and around crowds — that infection and fungal risk is real.
questions.expected_answer: Wear a mask in public and around crowds when your counts are low, until the team says you can stop.

questions.question: What is the exact fever rule for when you need to call us?
questions.source_quote: call for any single temperature over 100.4, or a temperature between 100.0 and 100.4 that lasts an hour or more.
questions.expected_answer: Call for any single temperature over 100.4, or a temperature of 100.0–100.4 lasting an hour or more.

questions.question: Why shouldn't you take Tylenol when you feel feverish?
questions.source_quote: Don't wait, don't take Tylenol to hide any of it — call.
questions.expected_answer: Tylenol can hide a fever; do not take it to cover symptoms — call the team instead.

questions.question: If you get chills or start shaking but have no fever, what should you do?
questions.source_quote: if you get chills or start shaking, what we call rigors, call us. That shaking can be a sign of a serious infection even before the thermometer catches up.
questions.expected_answer: Call the team right away — chills or shaking (rigors) can signal serious infection even without a fever.

questions.question: What signs of GVHD or serious problems should make you call us right away?
questions.source_quote: Call us right away for: a skin rash, especially on your palms or soles, new diarrhea, yellowing of your eyes or skin, or a fever.
questions.expected_answer: A skin rash (especially palms or soles), new diarrhea, yellowing of eyes or skin, or a fever.

questions.question: Can you explain what GVHD is and why these two medicines are important?
questions.source_quote: after your transplant, your new donor cells can sometimes attack your own body. That's called graft-versus-host disease, or GVHD. These two medicines are how we prevent that.
questions.expected_answer: GVHD is when donor cells attack your own body; tacrolimus and CellCept help prevent it.
